course_description: Theory and practice of economic policy. Fiscal & monetary policy in open economy. Fixed versus flexible exchange rates. Economic interdependence and international business cycles. Growth and development policy. Money and inflation in Australia. Political business cycles, central bank independence and inflation targeting. Fiscal policy and inflation. Labour markets and unemployment.

requirements.assumed_background: It is assumed that students will have previously covered material in the area of Macroeconomics at the introductory level and also some study at the intermediate level e.g. a sound knowledge of the closed economy ISLM model, open economy ISLM model, AD-AS modeling framework and the Neoclassical growth model.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Tutorials for this course begin in Teaching Week 2. Students should sign up for tutorials using mySi-net. Sign-on will be available from 10am Wednesday 15 July until 5pm Friday 14 August .

Please note there will be no classes on Wednesday 12th August - RNA Show Holiday.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of this course is to further provide students with an understanding of the key relationships and causal links between well-defined major macroeconomic aggregates. This develops an understanding of the role and workings of economic policy and provides a coherent, analytical framework from which to critically evaluate these policies. The exposition of economic policy in this course is quite general in nature, although a concerted effort is made to focus the course material selectively on some of the major economic issues important in the contemporary Australian context.

learning_outcomes.description: Command and utilise a core, comprehensive and well-founded knowledge of the field of modern macroeconomics, in particular, international linkages and influences on the Australian macroeconomy.
Better understand applied macroeconomic issues, and knowledge and understanding of economic data.
Have the ability to define and analyse problems based on modeling frameworks and an understanding of relevant data and institutional detail.
Identify key contemporary macroeconomic problems, both domestic and international and discuss possible solutions and potential innovations towards improving current policies and practices.
Collect, analyse, and organise information and ideas independently through wider reading and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently in both written and spoken forms.
The course aims to further develop students analytical skills and to encourage lateral thinking.

SCHOOL OF ECONOMICS CALCULATOR POLICY

The School of Economics Calculator Policy (updated 8 January 2008) states that from Semester 1 2008, Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) are the ONLY calculators permitted to be used in School of Economics examinations, both mid semester and final examinations. Any other calculator brought into the examination room will be confiscated.

PLEASE NOTE: This policy applies to all students enrolled in ECON coded courses.

REFERENCING GUIDELINES

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site www.uq.edu.au/economics
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Useful supplements to this text (including highly relevant practice materials) are available on the internet at: www.mhhe.com/au/dornbusch2e . Students are strongly encouraged to examine this supplement.

To obtain the username and password to access the student section of these online supplementary materials a student will need a registration code, which is included on the card at the front of the textbook. If a student does not wish to purchase the book but still wishes to access the online content then they are able to purchase an access code either through the bookstore, or by contacting McGraw-Hill customer service (phone 1800 646 572, or cservice_sydney@mcgraw-hill.com).

Every effort will be made to make copies of detailed lecture notes, the lecturers overheads, tutorial questions and answer guides, additional recommended readings and practice materials available to students on the ECON2040 website on Blackboard in timely fashion. However, students must attempt to attend lectures and tutorials, to take detailed lecture notes during lecture times and to read the required text and other required reading materials.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Lecture Topic 1: Policy and Economic Growth (Lecture): Neoclassical versus endogenous growth; economic convergence debate; two sector models and poverty traps; population growth; public and social infrastructure; natural resources and recycling; the Asian Tigers; the Truly Poor countries. Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Journal article readings, notes and tutorial); DBCFS2e (Chapter 4)
Lecture Topic 2 : Normative Policy Making (Lecture): a. Target and Instruments; b. Lags and uncertainty; c. Rational Expectations and the Lucas Critique;

Lecture Topic 3: The Political Business Cycle (Lecture): The positive theory of policymaking; elections and the macroeconomy; 'it's the economy stupid!'; political and partisan cycles. Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Journal article readings, notes and tutorial); DBCFS2e (Chapter 19)
Midsemester Examination (Exam) Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Relevant materials from Lecture topics 1-3 inclusive); DBCFS2e (Relevant chapters from lecture topics 1-3 inclusive)

Lecture Topic 4: Further on Fiscal Policy (Lecture): Fiscal policy and the government budget; Ricardian approach to deficits and sustainability; intertemporal tax smoothing. Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Notes and tutorial); DBCFS2e (Chapter 12, 13)

Lecture Topic 5: Inflation and Monetary Policy (Lecture): Further on Inflation and Monetary Policy in the Australian Context Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Reserve Bank website readings, journal article readings, notes and tutorial); DBCFS2e (Chapter 17)

Lecture Topic 6: Issues in the Open Economy (Lecture): Further Issues in the Open Economy continued - Exchange rates, the J-curve; Intertemporal analysis of the current account; international economic interdependence; the international business cycle. Readings/Ref: Blackboard ((Notes and tutorial)); DBCFS2e (Chapter 11)

Lecture Topic 7: Labour Markets and Unemployment (Lecture): Australia's unemployment problem; measuring unemployment; cyclical unemployment versus the natural rate; sticky wage models versus search and matching (flow) approaches; unemployment policy; the Five Economists plan; the recent reforms. Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Notes and tutorial); DBCFS2e (Chapter 18)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111 ).

Where an adjustment is made to an accredited program, it is the responsibility of the relevant Faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of the change/s.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers topics in modern macroeconomic policy at the intermediate level and builds significantly on the material covered in the required prerequisite ECON2020 Macroeconomic Theory course at this level. The core aim of this course is to further provide students with an understanding of the key relationships and causal links between well-defined major macroeconomic aggregates. This develops an understanding of the role and workings of economic policy and provides a coherent, analytical framework from which to critically evaluate these policies. The exposition of economic policy in this course is quite general in nature, although a concerted effort is made to focus the course material selectively on some of the major economic issues important in the contemporary Australian context.

Emphasis will be placed on economic policymaking and the use and understanding of coherent and consistent frameworks in which to analyse the workings of the macroeconomy and on which to build and critique economic policy. Time permitting, topics will include: instruments and targets of economic policy, time consistency of policy, microfoundations of modern macroeconomics, policy and economic growth, issues in macroeconomic stabilisation and policy effectiveness, rational expectations, unemployment and the labour market, intertemporal models of the government sector and of the open economy, inflation and monetary policy in the Australian context, fiscal policy, budget deficits, public debt and tax reform and political and partisan cycles.

Students should be aware that the trend in macroeconomics is towards understanding aggregate economic activity through examination of models with well-defined, consistent foundations at the microeconomic level. Thus, students should understand that there is a fundamental interconnection between macroeconomics and microeconomics. Some elementary concepts from microeconomics will be used in this course. Students should also be aware that simple algebra and elementary calculus will be used in the development and explanation of economic frameworks. Students should expect to see economic analysis explained graphically, mathematically and in words and are expected to work towards an understanding of economic models as a means of comprehending the role and effectiveness of economic policy.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .

C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .

D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
You are strongly encouraged to attend all of the teaching sessions. The lectures and tutorials have been specifically designed to aid your learning of the course material. Failure to attend either a lecture or a tutorial session may result in you being seriously disadvantaged. In particular, tutorial attendance is strongly recommended as an important supplement to lecture attendance. In the tutorials you will be able to clarify important points that you have not yet grasped. You will also practice the model solving and model manipulation skills you will need to do the mid-semester and final exams.

Note that tutorial attendance will be kept systematically. Students should be aware that additional information concerning their tutorial attendance will be used in making final judgements concerning the allocation of grades for those students whose formal assessment leaves them at the margin of obtaining a higher grade.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Complete failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge and understanding of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 0-19 percent inclusive.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 20-44 percent inclusive.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Some basic knowledge is evident, but detail and precision are patently absent at a level sufficient to merit competency and therefore a passing grade. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 45-49 percent inclusive.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic knowledge of core concepts and modelling techniques obtained. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 50-64 percent inclusive.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A more thorough and accurate understanding of modelling and policy analysis displayed through both written and verbal means. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 65-74 percent inclusive.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

A thorough, accurate and comprehensive knowledge of concepts and model analysis displayed and some evidence of wider reading. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 75-84 percent inclusive.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Accuracy and attention to detail displayed in all analysis together with significant evidence of critical evaluation of material and of wider reading. Requires an overall mark of 85 percent and above.

Other Requirements & Comments:

Note that both lecture and tutorial attendance will be closely monitored in this course. Tutorial attendance will be kept. Students should be aware that additional information concerning their tutorial attendance will be used in making final judgements concerning the allocation of grades for those students whose formal assessment leaves them at the margin of obtaining a higher grade.
other_assessment_information:
At the end of the semester, a student's best score among the following three weighting schemes will be used by the Course Coordinator to allocate the student's overall grade for the course.

Scheme 1: Midsemester Exam 35%, Final Exam 65%
Scheme 2: Midsemester Exam 50%, Final Exam 50%
Scheme 3: Midsemester Exam 60%, Final Exam 40%

Note that both lecture and tutorial attendance will be closely monitored in this course. Tutorial attendance will be kept. Students should be aware that additional information concerning their tutorial attendance will be used in making final judgements concerning the allocation of grades for those students whose formal assessment leaves them at the margin of obtaining a higher grade.

SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time. Note that the mid-semester exam is a SCHOOL exam, and the final exam is a CENTRAL EXAM.

Information about special examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations Please read this information very carefully.

All applications for special exams must be submitted within (5) days of the original date of the examination.

Mid-Semester Examination (SCHOOL exam) - submit your application to the Course Administrator, Christina Ellsworth, with appropriate documentation attached. Do not submit applications to the Faculty Resource Centre or the Student Centre.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at : http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-examinations-2901 Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Special & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (16-18 December).
